response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Honestly, dealing with fake content on social media makes me quite nervous because you never really know what to trust anymore. I spend about an hour each day scrolling through my feeds here in Lecce, and I have to admit that all this false information creates a lot of anxiety for me. As someone who works with my hands as a plumber, I prefer things that are straightforward and real, not these complicated stories that turn out to be completely invented. When I see something that seems too dramatic or too perfect, I immediately feel suspicious. My approach is quite simple and maybe old-fashioned, but it works for me. I try to stick with sources I already know, like local news from Lecce or official pages from my mosque. If something seems strange or makes me feel uneasy, I just do not share it, even if my friends have posted it. I have learned the hard way that sharing without checking can cause problems, and I already have enough worries in my life without adding more. What really bothers me is when fake news touches on religious matters or tries to create fear about Islam. I have seen too many false stories that misrepresent my faith, and this makes me very cautious about everything I read. I usually ask myself whether the information comes from a reliable source before I believe it. Sometimes I will check with people I trust at the mosque or even call my mother, who despite only finishing primary school, has good common sense about these things. My father had a PhD and always told me to question things, though I never got much formal education myself. I think the best defense against fake content is simply to slow down. When you are anxious like me, your first instinct might be to worry about every scary headline, but I have learned to take a breath, maybe go for a ride on my bicycle to clear my head, and then look at the information again with fresh eyes. I do not engage much with strangers online anyway since I am more of a quiet person, so I avoid getting into arguments about what is real or fake. For me, the key is limiting my exposure, trusting familiar sources, and not rushing to share everything I see. It is not a perfect system, but it helps me maintain some peace of mind in this confusing digital world.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 50-year-old self-employed plumber from Lecce with high neuroticism (anxious), low openness (prefers familiar, traditional approaches), low extraversion (quiet, not socially engaged), and no formal education. His anxiety would make fake content particularly distressing. His practical, hands-on occupation suggests he values tangible, verifiable information. His Islamic faith and experience with misrepresentation would make him sensitive to religious fake news. His limited social media use (1 hour daily) and introverted nature mean he's not deeply embedded in social media culture. His hobbies like cycling provide outlets for stress relief. The contrast between his parents' education levels (primary school vs PhD) suggests exposure to both practical wisdom and critical thinking. As a widower with no children, he may rely on community (mosque) and family (mother) for validation. The tone should be cautious, somewhat worried, practical, and straightforward, reflecting his anxiety and preference for simplicity.
response_parsed.tone: cautious, anxious, practical, straightforward, somewhat worried
